results.possibleIdentity: Robert 'Bob' Whitman (1935–2024) was a pioneering American multimedia and performance artist. He was a central figure in the 'Happenings' movement of the late 1950s and early 1960s, alongside artists such as Allan Kaprow and Claes Oldenburg. In 1966, Whitman co-founded Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) with artist Robert Rauschenberg and Bell Labs engineers Billy Klüver and Fred Waldhauer. The organization was dedicated to fostering collaborations between the arts and scientific communities. Whitman's work often integrated new technologies, such as his 1966 performance 'Two Holes of Water - 3' for the seminal '9 Evenings: Theatre & Engineering' event, which used cars as projection booths. During the late 1960s and early 1970s, he experimented with lasers (e.g., 'Wavy Red Line' in 1967) and telecommunications projects like 'NEWS' (1972), where participants called in reports from payphones to be broadcast live. He also played a key role in designing the Pepsi Pavilion for Expo '70 in Osaka, which featured a massive spherical mirror. His career spanned over six decades, continuing into the 21st century with projects utilizing cellphones and digital media.

results.possibleIdentity: Juan Downey (1940–1993) was a Chilean-born artist and architect recognized as a pioneer in video art, interactive art, and electronic sculpture. Active prominently in the late 1960s and throughout the 1970s, he was a key figure in the intersection of art and technology. After studying architecture in Chile and printmaking in Paris, Downey moved to the United States in 1965, living in Washington, D.C., before settling in New York City in 1969. He was deeply involved in the New York art and technology scene, participating in the 'Some More Beginnings' exhibition organized by Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) at the Brooklyn Museum in 1968. Downey's work often explored cybernetics, feedback systems, and what he termed 'invisible architectures'—interconnected networks of information and energy. He was also an early member of the Raindance collective and contributed to the influential journal Radical Software. His most notable works include 'Video Trans Américas' (1973–1976), a project involving extensive travels through Latin America to record indigenous cultures, and 'Plato Now' (1973), an interactive video performance. He served as an associate professor at the Pratt Institute from 1970 until his death.

results.possibleIdentity: Phil Deal (also identified as Phillip Deal) was a conceptual and performance artist active in the late 1960s and 1970s. He was involved in the San Francisco art scene, where he was associated with Project Artaud, an artist-run collective. He operated the Phil Deal Performance Gallery in San Francisco. His work often involved interdisciplinary collaborations; for example, he collaborated with artist Gail Bishop on projects like 'Southwest,' which combined photography with paintings and drawings. His profile as an artist working with conceptual and performance-based media aligns with the types of projects often facilitated by Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), which promoted the intersection of art and scientific/technical methods.

Phillip Deal is an artist and physicist who graduated with a degree in Nuclear Physics in 1973. He identifies as a 'Photo Artist' and creator of 'concept art.' His background in both a rigorous scientific discipline and the visual arts makes him a likely candidate for association with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), an organization specifically founded to foster collaborations between artists and scientists. He has also been published as a writer, with works including 'Love In An Iron Bowl,' and holds an MFA in fiction writing from Wichita State University.

Paul H. Deal was a scientist at NASA's Ames Research Center active in the late 1960s and early 1970s. While primarily a life scientist (authoring papers such as 'Altered gravitational field effects on the fertilized frog egg' in 1970), he has been noted for his interest in 'concept art' and the creation of early computer simulations and games (such as the text RPG 'Fallthru'). Given that E.A.T. coordinated a major project titled 'The Artist in the Research Laboratory' in 1969, which involved artists visiting NASA Ames to work with scientists, it is possible P. Deal was one of the technical collaborators who worked within this artist-engineer framework.
